result:
I've been thinking about the strange beauty of fog lately. There's something transformative about how it rolls in and softens the edges of everything—buildings become suggestions, trees turn into watercolor brushstrokes, and the familiar world takes on an almost dreamlike quality.

Fog forces us to slow down and pay attention differently. Without the usual visual anchors, we navigate by sound and intuition. Footsteps echo oddly. Voices carry in unexpected ways. The screech of unseen birds seems both closer and farther than it should be. It's as if the normal rules of space temporarily dissolve.

I find it fascinating how fog can make the mundane feel mysterious. A simple streetlight becomes a glowing orb floating in gray nothingness. An ordinary park bench transforms into something vaguely ominous when you can only see its outline. Even your own neighborhood can feel foreign and unexplored.

There's also something deeply humbling about fog—this reminder that nature can still make our human constructs disappear, at least visually. All our buildings and roads and signs can be swallowed up by something as simple as water droplets suspended in air. It's temporary, of course, but while it lasts, fog gives us a glimpse of how the world might look without us, or how it might have looked before us.

Maybe that's why I find foggy mornings so compelling. They're like brief visits to an alternate dimension where everything is quieter, softer, and full of hidden possibilities.
